section: Chapter: Tribals, Dikus and the Vision of a Golden Age

questions.question: Who are tribals?
questions.correct_answer: Tribals are indigenous communities who traditionally live in forests, hills and remote areas.
questions.explanation: They have their own culture, traditions and ways of living closely connected to nature.

questions.question: What does the term 'Adivasi' mean?
questions.correct_answer: Adivasi means the original inhabitants of a region.

questions.question: What does the word 'Diku' mean?
questions.correct_answer: Diku refers to outsiders who exploited tribal communities.
questions.explanation: Tribal people used this term for moneylenders, traders and landlords.

questions.question: What was the main occupation of many tribal communities?
questions.correct_answer: Hunting, gathering, farming and forest-based activities.
questions.explanation: Tribal livelihoods were closely connected to forests and natural resources.

questions.question: Who was Birsa Munda?
questions.correct_answer: Birsa Munda was a tribal leader who led a revolt against British rule.
questions.explanation: He inspired tribal communities to fight against exploitation.

questions.question: What was the 'Ulgulan' movement?
questions.correct_answer: Ulgulan was the tribal rebellion led by Birsa Munda.
questions.explanation: It aimed to end British rule and restore tribal rights.

questions.question: Why did tribals revolt against the British?
questions.correct_answer: Because of exploitation, loss of land and restrictions on forest use.
questions.explanation: British policies disturbed their traditional way of life.

questions.question: What was the impact of British forest laws on tribals?
questions.correct_answer: They restricted tribal access to forests and resources.
questions.explanation: This affected their livelihood and traditions.

questions.question: What did Birsa Munda dream of?
questions.correct_answer: A golden age where tribal people could live freely without exploitation.
questions.explanation: He wanted to restore tribal traditions and rights.

questions.question: When did Birsa Munda lead his rebellion?
questions.explanation: His movement gained strength in the 1890s.

notes.title: Tribal Communities
notes.content: Tribal communities lived in forests and depended on nature for their livelihood through hunting, gathering and farming.

notes.title: Dikus
notes.content: The term Diku was used by tribal people for outsiders such as traders, landlords and British officials who exploited them.

notes.title: Birsa Munda
notes.content: Birsa Munda was an important tribal leader who led a movement against British rule and exploitation of tribal communities.

notes.title: Ulgulan Movement
notes.content: The Ulgulan movement was a tribal uprising that aimed to protect tribal land and culture.

notes.title: British Forest Policies
notes.content: British forest laws restricted tribal access to forests, affecting their livelihoods and traditional practices.

facts.fact: Birsa Munda became a symbol of tribal resistance against British rule.
The Ulgulan movement was also known as the 'Great Tumult'.
Tribal revolts were often triggered by loss of land and forest rights.
British forest laws greatly affected traditional tribal lifestyles.
